1: The Jerusalem Council (ca. 48-49 CE) addressed the most critical ecclesiological question of the first generation: must Gentiles become Jews (via circumcision and Torah observance) to be Christians? The Pharisaic position (Acts 15:5) was consistent with the synagogue practice for gerim (resident aliens) who underwent full conversion. Peter's response (Acts 15:7-11) deployed the Cornelius precedent (8-10 years earlier) as the Spirit's own verdict. James's ruling (Acts 15:13-21) cited Amos 9:11-12 in LXX form (which differs significantly from the MT, reading 'the rest of humanity' instead of 'the remnant of Edom') as the scriptural warrant for Gentile inclusion without circumcision.
20: The Jerusalem Council's four prohibitions (apechesthai eidolotytōn kai haimatos kai pniktōn kai porneias: abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from strangled animals, and from sexual immorality) are best understood as the Noahide commands (Genesis 9:1-17) as formalized in later rabbinic tradition (b. Sanhedrin 56a: seven Noahide laws include prohibitions on idolatry, sexual immorality, and blood). The decree does not require full Torah observance but marks the minimum for table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers.
2: Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and debate (staseōs kai zētēseōs ouk oligēs) with the men from Judea who insisted on circumcision: stasis is the word for civic faction or riot — the controversy was serious and disruptive to the Antioch community. The decision to send Paul, Barnabas, and others to Jerusalem reflects the Antioch church's recognition that this question required adjudication by the apostolic center. The authority of Jerusalem over diaspora churches (inherited from synagogue network structure) is both assumed and contested in this episode.
3: Being sent on their way by the church, they passed through Phoenicia and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles and bringing great joy to all the brothers: the informal mission reports along the route preview the formal Jerusalem report. Phoenicia and Samaria had received the gospel in the first dispersion phase (Acts 11:19; Acts 8:1-5); their communities' joy at Gentile conversion implies that these communities were already theologically open to the Gentile mission — the resistance came specifically from the Jerusalem-Judea sphere.
4: When they arrived in Jerusalem, they were welcomed by the church and the apostles and the elders (tē te ekklēsia kai tois apostolois kai tois presbyterois): the triple reception group — congregation, apostles, elders — reflects the Jerusalem community's organizational structure. The apostles are still the senior authority; the elders (introduced in Acts 11:30) are the administrative governance body; the congregation is the communal witness. Paul and Barnabas reported all that God had done through them — the same report format as to Antioch (Acts 14:27).
5: Some believers who belonged to the party of the Pharisees (tines tōn apo tēs haireseōs tōn Pharisaiōn pepisteukotes) rose up and said it was necessary to circumcise the Gentile converts and to order them to keep the law of Moses: the Pharisaic party within the church represents the highest standard of Jewish Torah-observance in the first-century world. Their position was scripturally grounded (Genesis 17:10-14 — circumcision as the eternal covenant sign) and had deep theological precedent. The question was whether the eschatological arrival of Christ had modified the covenant's applicability to Gentile converts.
6: The apostles and elders gathered to consider this matter: the Jerusalem Council's formal deliberation structure mirrors the Sanhedrin — the body of recognized leaders gathered to decide a matter of religious law. The private deliberation (Acts 15:6, apostles and elders) precedes the larger assembly (Acts 15:12, the whole congregation). The council's decision carries authority not because of its institutional power alone but because James's exegetical argument (Acts 15:14-18) grounds it in Scripture.
7: After much debate (pollēs de zētēseōs genomenēs), Peter stood and said: the much debate is historically realistic — the question had been contested in the church since at least the Cornelius episode (Acts 11:1-18). Peter's intervention carries weight because of the Cornelius precedent: God had already decided the question by giving the Spirit to uncircumcised Gentiles before baptism, without requiring circumcision. Peter's speech translates the Cornelius experience into a theological principle: God himself tested the Gentiles' hearts and found faith, not Torah-observance, as the criterion.
8: 'God who knows the heart (kardiognōstēs ho theos) bore witness to them by giving them the Holy Spirit just as he did to us': kardiognōstēs appears only here and in Acts 1:24 in the NT — it is the divine attribute that makes God's acceptance-verdict final. God's knowledge of Cornelius's household's hearts, expressed through the Spirit's gift, is the evidence that circumcision is not the criterion for acceptance. The argument is entirely from divine action rather than human deliberation: God chose; the council should ratify God's choice.
9: 'He made no distinction (ouden diekrinen) between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith' — the cleansing of the heart by faith is the inward reality of which baptism is the outward sign; its occurrence in Cornelius's household before baptism establishes that the inward reality precedes and determines the validity of the outward rite. The ouden diekrinen (made no distinction) echoes Peter's own breakthrough in Acts 10:34 (ouk estin prosōpolēmptēs ho theos — God does not show partiality).
10: 'Why do you put God to the test by placing a yoke on the neck of the disciples that neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear?' (zugon... hon oute hoi pateres hēmōn oute hēmeis ischysamen bastasai): the yoke (zugos) metaphor for Torah-obligation was in common Jewish use (Sir Acts 51:26 — Ben Sira's wisdom as a yoke to bear). Peter's striking admission that the law is a 'burden' the Jewish community itself has not successfully borne reframes the question: imposing Torah-observance on Gentiles is testing God by requiring what God himself has not demanded as the salvation-condition.
11: Peter's thesis — 'we believe that we will be saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they will' (alla dia tēs charitos tou kyriou Iēsou pisteuomen sōthēnai kath hon tropon kakeinoi) — is the Pauline soteriology in Petrine form. The reversal is deliberate: not 'Gentiles saved like Jews' (by Torah) but 'Jews saved like Gentiles' (by grace through faith). This is the exegetical foundation that the Jerusalem Council endorses and that Paul will develop throughout his letters.
12: All the assembly fell silent and listened to Barnabas and Paul as they related what signs and wonders God had done through them among the Gentiles: the assembly's silence after Peter's speech is the rhetorical pause before the evidence. The signs and wonders (sēmeia kai terata) God performed through Paul and Barnabas among the Gentiles are the material corroboration of Peter's theological argument: God himself authenticated the Gentile mission through miracles, just as he authenticated the Jerusalem mission at Pentecost.
13: After they finished speaking, James replied: James the Lord's brother (identified in Galatians 1:19 and 2:9 as a pillar of the Jerusalem church) provides the decisive ruling. His authority in the Jerusalem church by this point (ca. 48-49 CE) is unquestioned — both Josephus (Ant. 20.197-203) and Paul (Galatians 2:12) confirm his leadership. His use of 'Simeon' (the Hebrew form of Peter's name) rather than 'Simon' or 'Peter' signals a deliberate formality in the citation.
14: 'Simeon has related how God first visited the Gentiles to take from them a people for his name' (labon ex ethnōn laon tō onomati autou): the language of 'taking a people' from the nations is the election vocabulary applied to Israel (Deuteronomy 7:6; Acts 14:2 — 'a people for his own possession'). James interprets the Cornelius event as the formation of a Gentile covenant-people — not Gentiles joining Israel but a parallel act of divine choosing. The phrase 'for his name' grounds Gentile inclusion in God's own covenantal purpose.
15: The words of the prophets agree with this (toutō symphōnousin hoi logoi tōn prophētōn): James grounds the decision in Scripture rather than in experience alone. The agreement of prophetic Scripture with the empirical evidence (Spirit falling on Gentiles, miraculous signs) is the double testimony that carries the council. James cites Amos 9:11-12 as the scriptural warrant — the passage about rebuilding David's fallen tent to enable the nations to seek the Lord.
16: Amos 9:11 LXX — 'I will return and rebuild the tent of David that has fallen' (anastrophō kai anoikodomēsō tēn skēnēn Dauid tēn pepokytian): James quotes the LXX version, which differs from the MT at the crucial point. The LXX reads 'the remnant of mankind may seek the Lord and all the Gentiles who are called by my name' where the MT reads 'the remnant of Edom.' The LXX's universalizing translation enabled the application to Gentile inclusion — either a prior translation decision that James finds useful, or evidence that this was the LXX tradition he knew.
17: The Gentile nations 'who are called by my name' (ep hous epikeklētai to onoma mou ep autous): the application of the divine-name formula to Gentiles echoes the Abrahamic covenant's universal scope (Genesis 12:3). James's exegesis reads the Amos passage as anticipating a time when Gentiles would be incorporated into the covenant community under God's name — not by becoming ethnic Israelites but by being 'called by the name' of Israel's God through faith. This is the scriptural grounding for the council's ruling.
18: 'Known to God from eternity are all his works' (gnōsta ap aiōnos): the eternity of divine foreknowledge grounds the current decision in God's eternal purpose rather than apostolic improvisation. The Gentile mission is not a contingency plan arising from Israel's failure but the unfolding of what God had 'made known from of old'. The council's ruling thus aligns with the divine plan rather than creating new policy — it recognizes what God has already decided and acted on.
19: James's ruling: 'my judgment is that we should not trouble (mē parenochleîn) those of the Gentiles who turn to God' — parenochleō (trouble/burden) is a bureaucratic term for imposing unnecessary administrative requirements. The ruling is not that Torah is wrong but that its full observance is not the condition for Gentile entry into the covenant community. James's authority to render a ruling (egō krinō, 'I judge/decide') is confirmed by the council's acceptance of his position.
21: James's rationale for the four prohibitions: 'Moses has had in every city those who proclaim him, for he is read every Sabbath in the synagogues' — the ongoing availability of Mosaic instruction in diaspora synagogues means Gentile converts have access to the fuller Torah through communal hearing, without requiring formal conversion. The four abstentions are the minimum Leviticus 17-18 requirements for resident aliens in Israel (ger); by requiring these, James places Gentile believers in the legal category of resident aliens in the covenant community.
22: Then it seemed good to the apostles and the elders, with the whole church, to choose men from among them and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas (eklesamenois andras ex autōn pempsai eis Antiocheian syn tō Paulō kai Barnaba): the communal decision-making — apostles, elders, and whole congregation together — is the Jerusalem church's governance model. Judas Barsabbas and Silas are chosen as authoritative delegates: their presence authenticates the letter as genuinely from Jerusalem, preventing Paul and Barnabas from misrepresenting the council's position (accidentally or otherwise).
23: The letter begins: 'The brothers, both the apostles and the elders, to the brothers who are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia' — the letter's address covers the Pauline mission territory: Antioch (the base), Syria (the surrounding region), and Cilicia (Paul's home region). The joint authorship — apostles and elders together — reflects the council's institutional structure. This is one of the few actual documents cited (if not verbatim) in Acts, giving it historical-source character.
24: 'We have heard that some persons have gone out from us and troubled you with words, unsettling your minds, although we gave them no instructions' (ois ou diesteilametha): the letter's disavowal of the circumcision teachers is diplomatically critical — it removes their authority claim without naming them. By stating that these teachers had no commission from Jerusalem, the council prevents the Antioch church from feeling required to comply with their demand. The verb diastello (instruct/command) is the word for official directive — they had no official directive.
25: The letter affirms the decision reached: 'it has seemed good to us, having come to one accord' (homothymadon) — the unanimity-term that characterized the original Jerusalem community (Acts 1:14; Acts 2:1, 46; Acts 4:24; Acts 5:12). The delegation of Barnabas and Paul as 'beloved men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ' is the council's formal commendation of the first-journey missionaries — establishing their credibility with the Antioch church and beyond.
26: Judas and Silas are sent to confirm the letter verbally: ancient diplomatic practice required oral confirmation of written documents by authorized messengers who could speak to the document's authenticity and expand on its contents. Diastellō (convey/explain in detail, Acts 15:27) — the delegates will provide oral elaboration of what the letter summarizes. The dual channel — written decree plus live messengers — ensures that the ruling cannot be misunderstood or misrepresented.
27: Judas and Silas will tell you the same things by word of mouth: oral tradition supplementing written documents was the standard practice in both Jewish and Greco-Roman administrative contexts. Letters were often accompanied by trusted messengers who could authenticate, expand, and respond to questions about the document. Silas will become Paul's companion on the second journey (Acts 15:40), suggesting his Antioch stay developed into a longer relationship with the Pauline mission community.
28: 'It has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us' (edoxen gar tō pneumati tō hagiō kai hēmin): the council's formula for its decision is remarkable — the Spirit and the human council are named jointly as the decision's authors. This is neither pure democratic consensus nor pure charismatic revelation; it is the council's understanding that the Spirit's prior action (Cornelius, the first-journey signs) confirmed what their scriptural deliberation has now ratified. The coupling of Spirit and council is the model for subsequent conciliar authority claims.
29: The four prohibitions are stated: abstain from things sacrificed to idols, from blood, from things strangled, and from sexual immorality (porneia). Leviticus 17-18 provides the framework: Leviticus 17:8-9 (sacrifices), Acts 17:10-14 (blood), Acts 17:15-16 (carrion/strangled animals), Acts 18:6-23 (sexual prohibitions). These were the Torah's requirements for gerim (resident aliens) living among Israelites — placing Gentile believers in this legal category acknowledged their covenant participation without requiring full proselyte conversion.
30: They went down to Antioch and gathered the congregation (synagagontes to plēthos) and delivered the letter: the public reading of the Jerusalem letter before the gathered assembly is the formal promulgation of the conciliar decision. The whole community hears the ruling — not just the leaders. The public character of the announcement ensures broad accountability: the Antioch community is now collectively informed and cannot claim ignorance of the Jerusalem church's position.
31: When they had read it, they rejoiced because of its encouragement (echarēsan epi tē paraklēsei): the community's joy at the letter signals relief — the circumcision controversy had been a source of serious tension (Acts 15:2 — 'no small dissension'). The letter's encouragement is specifically the liberation from the burden of circumcision requirement; the Gentile believers are affirmed in their status without needing to become cultural Jews. The resolution of the first major internal theological controversy in the church produces joy.
32: Judas and Silas, who were themselves prophets, encouraged and strengthened the brothers with many words (logō pollō pareklEsan tous adelphous kai epestērixan): prophets in the NT had both predictive and pastoral-exhortatory functions (cf. Agabus, Acts 11:28; Acts 21:10-11). Judas and Silas's prophetic ministry extends beyond delivering the letter to direct pastoral instruction. Epestērixan (strengthened) is the same verb Paul uses for his return visits to the first-journey churches (Acts 14:22; Acts 15:41) — a standard pastoral care vocabulary.
33: After spending some time (poiēsantes de chronon), they were sent off in peace by the brothers to those who had sent them: the return of Judas and Silas to Jerusalem completes the diplomatic mission. The 'sent off in peace' (apelythesan met eirēnēs) is the standard Jewish and Christian farewell blessing — shalom / peace — that authenticates the conclusion of a successful mission. The Antioch-Jerusalem relationship, strained by the circumcision controversy, is restored by the council's ruling.
34: Verse 34 (noting that Silas decided to remain) is absent from the oldest manuscripts (P45, P74, Vaticanus, Sinaiticus) and is a scribal addition attempting to explain how Silas was available to accompany Paul in Acts 15:40, after Acts 15:33 says he was sent back to Jerusalem. The best reading treats Acts 15:34 as a later gloss; the narrative is intelligible without it — Silas either returned to Antioch or was elsewhere in the region when Paul sought a travel companion.
35: Paul and Barnabas remained in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord with many others (meta kai heterōn pollōn): the Antioch church is depicted as a teaching community with multiple leaders — not dependent on any single figure. The 'many others' (heteroi polloi) suggests an active network of teachers beyond the named apostles. The Antioch base of operations, with its mixed Jewish-Gentile composition and prophetic-teaching leadership (Acts 13:1), is the model for the churches Paul plants.
36: Paul's proposal to revisit the first-journey churches: 'let us return and visit the brothers in every city where we proclaimed the word of the Lord and see how they are' — the second-journey impulse is pastoral, not primarily evangelistic. The apostolic mission involves both planting (first journey) and watering/strengthening (second journey). The care for existing communities rather than always pioneering new territory reflects Paul's own self-understanding (Romans 15:20 — he preferred pioneer work but also nurtured existing communities).
37: Barnabas wanted to take with them John called Mark (Iōannēn ton kaloumenon Markon): Mark (John Mark) had withdrawn from the first journey at Pamphylia (Acts 13:13) — the reason is not given in Acts; 2 Timothy 4:11 (Paul later acknowledges Mark's usefulness) suggests the break was mended. Mark was Barnabas's cousin (Colossians 4:10), which explains Barnabas's loyalty. The family connection and Barnabas's characteristic encouragement disposition (Acts 4:36) made him willing to give Mark a second chance.
38: Paul did not think it wise to take with them one who had withdrawn (apostanta ap autōn apo Pamphylias) from the work: Paul's concern is mission integrity — a companion who withdrew under pressure is a liability in dangerous territory. The verb apistanai (withdraw/desert) is the same term used for apostasy in Hebrews 3:12 — though not implying that level of gravity here, it signals that Paul's objection is to the precedent of abandoning assigned work. The disagreement between Paul and Barnabas is an honest account of a real conflict.
39: There arose a sharp disagreement (paroxysmos) so that they separated from each other: paroxysmos (sharp contention, paroxysm) is a strong word — the same root as the English 'paroxysm'. The separation of Paul and Barnabas is neither glossed over nor condemned by Luke; it is presented as a historical fact whose providential result was two separate mission teams covering more territory. Barnabas took Mark to Cyprus (his home island, Acts 4:36); the Cyprus mission continues without Paul. Mark's rehabilitation is confirmed by Colossians 4:10 and 2 Timothy 4:11.
40: Paul chose Silas and departed, having been commended by the brothers to the grace of the Lord (paradotheis tē chariti tou kyriou hypo tōn adelphōn): the commissioning formula mirrors Acts 14:26 (the first-journey commission) — Paul and Silas depart as an officially sent mission team, not as freelancers. Silas's presence as a Jerusalem delegate (Acts 15:22, 27) gives the new team both Antiochene and Jerusalem credentialing. The 'grace of the Lord' is the divine resource entrusted to them — the same phrase as Barnabas and Paul's original commission.
41: He went through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches (epistērizōn tas ekklesIas): the second journey begins not with new territory but with pastoral consolidation of existing communities. Syria and Cilicia were Paul's home region (Galatians 1:21 — he mentions missionary activity there between his Damascus conversion and the Jerusalem Council). The 'churches' of Syria and Cilicia are communities that predate this visit; Paul is strengthening what was already there, not planting for the first time.
